service: Regulate provisioning of public telecommunications services on City streets.
oneLiner: This work matters because when the City retired its aging network of thousands of payphones in 2014, it had to negotiate a new franchise, the one that became LinkNYC, to make sure whoever wired the sidewalks next actually served the public rather than just running ads.
summary: OTI oversees the franchise agreements that let companies install telecommunications infrastructure, like kiosks, cables, and antennas, in the public right-of-way along City streets. The best-known example is LinkNYC, launched after a 2014 competitive bid replaced the City's roughly 9,000 to 13,000 public payphones with free Wi-Fi kiosks; the winning CityBridge consortium builds and owns the infrastructure under a 12-year franchise rather than the City paying for it directly, funding the network in part through advertising.

sources.label: NYC DoITT - LinkNYC Public Communications Structure Franchise Agreement
